name: Pilot Range
short_description: BLM public land rockhounding in the high desert of northern Utah. Collect quartz, agate, and jasper specimens with 25 lbs daily limit and no permit required.
description:
Pilot Range sits in Box Elder County's remote high desert landscape, offering excellent opportunities for recreational rockhounds seeking quality quartz and chalcedony specimens. At 6,339 feet elevation, this moderately sloped terrain transitions between sagebrush flats and rocky outcrops typical of the Great Basin geological province. The BLM-managed land welcomes personal collectors with straightforward rules and no permitting requirements, making this an accessible destination for both beginners and experienced rockhounders.

The geology of Pilot Range reflects its location within the Basin and Range province, where Cenozoic extension created fault-block mountains and valleys filled with alluvial and volcanic materials. Silica-rich hydrothermal fluids from ancient volcanic activity deposited secondary minerals including agate nodules, chalcedony veins, and quartz crystals in host rocks and stream gravels. These minerals concentrated in certain wash areas and hillside exposures, making systematic searching productive.

Visitors should expect moderate-difficulty terrain with scattered rocks, loose footing in places, and exposure to sun and wind at this elevation. The collecting experience rewards patience and careful observation of color variations in the alluvium and exposed bedrock. Water is scarce, so bring adequate supplies. The site works well for families and beginners willing to hike moderate distances from parking areas.

Productive collecting typically occurs in dry washes where gravity has naturally concentrated heavier specimens, and along hillsides where weathering exposes fresh material. The moderate slope provides good surface exposure without extreme climbing difficulty. Specimen quality varies, but patient collectors often find polishable agates and attractive quartz pieces.
history: Pilot Range sits within the Basin and Range physiographic province, where Cenozoic-age extension and normal faulting created the characteristic mountainous terrain. Miocene and Pliocene volcanic and hydrothermal activity deposited silica-rich fluids that created the secondary quartz, agate, and chalcedony deposits found throughout the range today.
tips: Focus your search in dry washes and alluvial fans where heavier specimens concentrate naturally. Look for translucent to opaque quartz with subtle color variations, banded agate patterns, and chalcedony in cream, tan, and reddish hues. Bring a rock pick to test specimens in outcrop areas, and use a 5-gallon bucket to transport finds. Start near lower elevation sections where erosion is most active, then work upslope if access permits.
terrain_notes: Elevation 6,339 feet with moderate slopes and scattered sagebrush vegetation typical of the Great Basin. Terrain is rocky with loose stone footing in many areas. Washes provide easier walking with natural pathways. Parking pullouts may be 0.5 to 2 miles from productive collecting areas depending on specific access point chosen. Expect variable ground conditions: some smooth alluvium, some sharp volcanic rock.
best_season: May - October. Spring (May-June) and early fall (September-October) offer moderate temperatures and lower water runoff risk. Summer (July-August) brings intense heat at 6,300+ feet elevation; plan early morning sessions. Winter snow makes access difficult and unpredictable.
written_directions: From Brigham City, take US 89 north approximately 18 miles toward Idaho. Turn east on local county roads into the Box Elder County foothills toward the Pilot Range area. Specific BLM access points are marked with range signs. GPS coordinates 41.248112, -114.039671 reference main collecting areas. Confirm current road conditions with Box Elder County or local BLM office before visiting, as high desert roads can become impassable after rain or snow.
road_conditions: Unimproved county roads with washboard and potential high-clearance requirements. Not recommended for low-clearance passenger vehicles, especially after rain. Conditions vary seasonally.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas along access roads. No developed parking facilities. Expect limited capacity and unmarked spaces. Arrive early during peak season.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection or sale of specimens. No motorized excavation or digging equipment. No collecting in closed or restricted areas. Pack out all waste.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection or sale
No motorized excavation
No heavy equipment
Pack out all waste
Respect posted closures
nearest_services: Brigham City approximately 20 miles south - groceries, gas, restaurants, and lodging available.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven rocky footing, loose stone, and variable ground conditions. Walking distances of 0.5 to 2 miles from parking required to reach productive areas. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. Terrain slopes moderately but is not steep enough to present major climbing challenges for fit adults. High elevation (6,339 feet) may affect those with respiratory sensitivity.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Pilot Range, Utah?
faq.answer: Pilot Range is known for high-quality quartz, agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. You can find banded agate in cream and reddish tones, translucent quartz crystals, and colorful jasper pieces in the washes and alluvial deposits. Most specimens are polishable and suitable for display or lapidary work.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect at Pilot Range on BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal recreational collecting at Pilot Range. Utah BLM allows rockhounds to collect on their own with a simple 25 lb per day limit under FLPMA regulations. Just follow the personal use rules and avoid commercial collection.

faq.question: Is Pilot Range rockhounding near Brigham City good for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Pilot Range is beginner-friendly. The moderate terrain and straightforward collecting rules make it accessible to new rockhounds. Start in the dry washes where specimens are easier to spot, bring basic tools like a rock pick and bucket, and plan for 2-4 hours of searching. Kids ages 8 and up can participate with adult supervision.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding at Pilot Range, Utah?
faq.answer: May through October is ideal. Spring and early fall (May-June and September-October) offer comfortable temperatures and lower water runoff risk. Avoid summer (July-August) when high elevation heat is intense, and winter when snow and ice make access difficult. Early morning sessions are recommended year-round.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring to Pilot Range rockhounding?
faq.answer: Bring a rock pick for testing specimens in outcrop, a 5-gallon bucket or backpack for carrying finds, sturdy hiking boots, sun protection, and at least 2 liters of water per person. A loupe helps identify agate banding and quartz quality. A GPS device is helpful given the remote location and spotty cell service.

faq.question: How far is the drive from Brigham City to Pilot Range for rockhounding?
faq.answer: Pilot Range is approximately 20 miles north of Brigham City via US 89 and local county roads into Box Elder County foothills. The drive takes 30-45 minutes depending on road conditions. County roads are unimproved and require high-clearance vehicles; check conditions before visiting as washboard and seasonal closures are common.
